# Runbook — ITS_Review_Queue triage (working the human-review queue + the resolve verb) (Successor-Remediation, Op Stds §43)

<!-- BEGIN TREE-XREF (generated by scripts/build_runbook_xrefs.py — do not edit inside) -->
> **In the troubleshooting tree** ([printable guide](../troubleshooting/troubleshooting_guide.md) · dashboard `/troubleshoot`). This runbook resolves:
>   - Safety report — portal submission to sent weekly packet → Below-confidence / flagged items route to human review → “Items sit in the Review Queue past their SLA.”
<!-- END TREE-XREF -->

A §43 successor-remediation entry, written for the **Successor-Operator**: a trained
operator who runs Claude Code and reads Smartsheet rows + alert emails, but does **not**
read code. The §42 code-reader rationale lives in `shared/review_queue.py` (the add /
SLA helpers) and `operator_dashboard/act/review_ops.py` (the resolve verb).

## Purpose

`ITS_Review_Queue` (ITS — System workspace / "03 — Queues") is where the system routes
**ambiguity instead of failing silently**. When a daemon cannot safely finish something
on its own, it parks the item here for a human. Typical arrivals:

- **Low-confidence extractions** — an automated read fell below the confidence
  threshold, so a human confirms instead of the system guessing.
- **Refused / suspicious documents** — attachment or photo screening refused a file,
  an HMAC check failed, or an anomaly sentinel fired. These carry the **Security Flag**.
- **Fenced per-job failures** — one job's compile/filing failed while the rest
  succeeded; the daemon fenced that one item and queued it here (e.g. a weekly-compile
  fence, a PO stuck on an unknown vendor).
- **Advisory signals** — capacity margin warnings, reviewer-chain gap anomalies from
  watchdog Check D, picklist removals blocked by live references.

Nothing else in the system ever moves a row out of PENDING — resolving the queue is
**operator work**, and this runbook is how to do it.

## What one row means

The columns you triage on:

- **Item ID** — stable identifier, `<workstream>-<YYYYMMDD>-<HHMMSS>` (UTC). Watchdog
  Check A names stale items by this ID.
- **Workstream** — which lane owns the item (`safety_reports`, `po_materials`, …,
  `global` for system-wide signals like the watchdog's). One of the two resolve-verb
  filter axes.
- **Summary** — the one-line "why it landed". Recurring classes share a stable prefix
  (e.g. `weekly compile failed for job …`), which is the other filter axis
  (starts-with match).
- **Reason** — the standardized cause code (picklist): `low-confidence-extraction`,
  `ambiguous-classification`, `structured-output-edge`, `zero-data-window`,
  `mismatched-reference`, `security-trigger`, `policy-edge`, `manual`, `other`, plus
  the sender-trust codes (`header-soft-fail-trusted`, `sender-pending-verification`,
  `project-out-of-scope`).
- **Severity** — reuses the ITS_Errors scale; defaults WARN, security-flagged items
  are typically CRITICAL, advisory nudges INFO.
- **SLA Tier** — how fast the item expects attention: `4h` (safety intake), `24h`
  (RFQ drafts), `48h` (subcontract drafts). **Watchdog Check A** WARNs every morning
  on PENDING rows older than **2× their SLA**, naming the Item IDs — that is the
  "queue is going stale" nag.
- **Security Flag** — checked when a security screen refused the item or an anomaly
  sentinel fired. See the priority rule below.
- **Payload / Source File** — the structured detail a reviewer needs.
- **Status** — `PENDING` on write; terminal `APPROVED` / `REJECTED` via the dashboard
  verb. **Resolved At / Resolution Notes** are stamped by the verb (the resolving
  operator's identity rides in Resolution Notes).

## Security-flag rows are TOP priority

A row with **Security Flag checked** means the system suspects **adversarial input** —
a screened-out attachment, a failed signature check, or an anomaly-sentinel hit on
extracted content. Handle these first, and differently:

- **Do NOT open, download, or forward** any attachment or quarantined content the row
  points at. The refusal *is* the control — never try to force the original through.
- Read the Summary/Payload to identify the submitting account and item, then
  **escalate to Seth** (security is FIXED high-class). A cluster of security-flagged
  rows (many items, one sender, repeated HMAC failures) is more urgent, not less.
- Only after Seth's disposition do you resolve the row (usually REJECTED).

## Procedure

### The low-class repair — resolve PENDING rows (dashboard review/resolve)

**Act on the underlying item first** — approve/fix/re-run/escalate whatever the row is
about (each daemon's own runbook has the symptom blocks). The queue row is the *record*
of the ambiguity; resolving it just closes the loop so Check A stops counting it.

Where: dashboard **`/config`** page → **"Review queue (Class B · elevated)"** section
(route `POST /act/review/resolve`).

1. **A Workstream and/or Summary-prefix filter is REQUIRED** (Workstream is an exact
   match; Summary is a starts-with match). An unfiltered mass-resolve is refused by
   the code — you resolve a known class, never the whole queue.
2. **Click *preview* first.** Preview is a dry run: it counts the matching PENDING
   rows and writes nothing, so you can check the blast radius before committing.
3. Pick the resolution: **REJECTED** (noise / stale / superseded) or **APPROVED**
   (handled out-of-band). Add a short note — it lands in Resolution Notes with your
   attribution.
4. Commit with the elevated ceremony: re-enter the PIN + type `resolve-review`.
5. Only **PENDING** rows are touched (re-runs are harmless); **nothing is deleted** —
   the sheet stays the audit trail. Large sets are capped per run; run again to
   continue. An audit row (`review_rows_resolved`) records who did it.

> "Claude, the `weekly compile failed for job <X>` rows in ITS_Review_Queue are moot —
> the job was removed. Walk me through previewing the Summary-prefix filter and
> resolving them REJECTED on the dashboard."

### If Check A keeps WARNing

Check A re-WARNs daily while any PENDING row sits past 2× its SLA. That is the
intended nag, not a fault: work the oldest Item IDs it names. If a class keeps
re-arriving as fast as you resolve it, stop resolving and treat the *generator* as the
fault (find its Symptom block in the owning daemon's runbook, or escalate).

### Escalate-to-Seth condition

- **Any security-flagged row** (above) — disposition is Seth's call; your part is
  containment (don't open it) + the report.
- Anything whose *fix* falls in the four FIXED high-capability classes: the External
  Send Gate, secrets/auth, doctrine, or code/schema changes (e.g. a
  `mismatched-reference` caused by a picklist *definition*, a totals/HMAC fence).
- **A recurring class that returns after its documented repair**, or a row class no
  runbook documents (novel = Seth, per the both-rule).
- When in doubt about APPROVED vs REJECTED on anything contractual or customer-facing
  — ask; resolving a row never sends anything, but it does close the record.

## Owner

`@solutionsmith`. Item-specific repairs live in the owning workstream's §43 runbook;
this entry owns the queue semantics, the security-flag priority rule, and the
dashboard resolve verb.
